content.text: Agentic AI systems introduce attack surfaces that standard application security review processes are not designed to evaluate: prompt injection vectors embedded in user inputs, tool calls that bridge the agent to file systems or external APIs, LLM-generated outputs that flow into downstream systems without sanitisation, and credentials that developers embed in prompt files during local testing. Security review gates formalise the process of evaluating these risks before code is merged and deployed.

A security review gate is an enforced checkpoint in a software delivery pipeline that blocks code from advancing — either to the main branch or to the next deployment environment — until a defined set of security checks have passed. For agentic AI, these gates combine automated scanning (SAST, secret detection, dependency CVE scanning, prompt injection analysis) with a human security reviewer approval that evaluates threat model changes, permission scope changes, and new tool integrations.

The gate is enforced in a technical sense: the CI/CD system marks the pull request as blocked until every required check produces a green status. No reviewer can approve-and-merge while a required security check is red. This removes the social pressure pathway where security concerns get bypassed under delivery timelines, which is where the majority of agentic AI security failures originate.

Security review gates for agentic AI extend standard application security review with AI-specific checks: analysis of prompt templates for injection vulnerabilities, review of tool definitions for over-permissive scopes, evaluation of output sanitisation for LLM responses that flow into HTML renderers or SQL builders, and adversarial prompt evaluation suites that must pass as CI status checks.

Standard code review catches many classes of security bug, but reviewers are not systematically checking for prompt injection, credential embedding in prompt files, or tool permission over-scoping. These issues are invisible to reviewers who are not explicitly looking for them. Agentic AI systems are particularly vulnerable because the attack surface changes with every prompt template edit, every new tool registration, and every output path addition — changes that would not trigger security concern in a traditional code review.

Production incidents in agentic systems frequently trace back to gaps that a security review gate would have caught: an API key embedded in a system prompt committed to a repository, a tool that grants file-write access when the agent only needed file-read, a prompt template that interpolates raw user input without sanitisation, allowing an attacker to hijack the agent's next action. Each of these is preventable by a gate that runs before merge.

Compliance contexts add an additional requirement: regulated industries require documented evidence that security review occurred and was gated on specific criteria. Manual review notes in PR comments do not constitute auditable evidence; CI system pass/fail records with timestamps and reviewer identity do. Security review gates produce this evidence automatically.
Security issues are caught at the PR stage rather than discovered in production, reducing the cost and urgency of remediation significantly.
Every change passes the same automated checks regardless of which reviewer was assigned, eliminating inconsistent coverage due to reviewer expertise or attention.
CI system records provide timestamped, reviewer-attributed evidence of security review for compliance and post-incident analysis.
Automated gates catch prompt injection patterns, secret embedding, and over-permissive tool definitions that standard code review misses entirely.
Security incidents that originate in merged code produce high-severity pages; preventing them at merge time reduces incident frequency.
Developers get immediate, specific feedback on what security check failed and why, rather than a general note from a reviewer days later.
Automated SAST and prompt injection scanners produce false positives. A high false positive rate erodes developer trust and creates pressure to suppress results.
Security rules and adversarial eval suites require ongoing maintenance as the system evolves. Stale rules lose coverage and produce false confidence.
Each gate adds latency to the PR merge path. An adversarial eval suite taking 20 minutes adds 20 minutes to every relevant PR cycle.
Automated gates cannot evaluate all threat model changes. Human reviewer approval remains necessary for novel attack surfaces that automated rules do not yet cover.
Human approval gates require security reviewers with agentic AI expertise, which is still rare in most organisations.

The gate pipeline runs on every pull request that touches agent-related code or configuration. The CI system evaluates a set of required status checks; each check is a separate job that runs in parallel where dependencies allow.

The automated gate sequence is: (1) SAST scan with Semgrep using custom agentic-AI ruleset plus Bandit for Python unsafe function calls; (2) secret detection with gitleaks and trufflehog scanning the full diff including prompt template files; (3) dependency CVE check where the lock file is scanned against NVD and OSV databases; (4) adversarial eval where a pytest suite of red-team prompt cases must pass at a 95% threshold. Each check produces a pass/fail status check visible in the PR interface.

The human approval gate requires a security-trained reviewer to complete a structured checklist covering threat model delta review, permission scope review, output handling review, and adversarial eval confirmation. The reviewer's checklist completion is recorded as a PR review approval, which branch protection rules require before merge is permitted.

attrs.alt: Security Review Gate Pipeline: automated scan gates feeding human reviewer approval gate

content.text: The pipeline runs SAST, secret scan, dependency CVE check, and adversarial eval in parallel (each as a separate CI job). All must pass before the human security reviewer approval gate becomes available. Red dashed lines indicate merge-blocking failure paths. The gate configuration itself is protected by CODEOWNERS routing to the security team.

Security review gates are appropriate for any agentic AI system that operates in a production environment, processes external user inputs, has access to privileged APIs or file systems, or is deployed in a regulated industry. The gate configuration should be calibrated to the risk profile of the system.

For internal-only agents without external inputs and with read-only tool access, a lighter gate configuration is appropriate: SAST and secret detection but not the full adversarial eval suite. For customer-facing agents with tool access to external services, the full gate pipeline including adversarial eval and human reviewer approval is required. The team should document the rationale for any gate scope reduction.

Security review gates are not appropriate as a substitute for foundational security architecture decisions. A gate cannot compensate for an agent that is architecturally over-permissioned or that lacks output sanitisation as a design principle — it can only flag deviations from a secure baseline.
Phase 1: Baseline CI pipeline with SAST, secrets, and dependency CVE

# .github/workflows/security-gates.yml
name: Security Review Gates

on:
  pull_request:
    branches: [main, staging]

jobs:
  sast-scan:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
        with:
          fetch-depth: 0
      - name: Run Semgrep SAST
        uses: semgrep/semgrep-action@v1
        with:
          config: >
            p/security-audit
            p/python
            .semgrep/agentic-rules.yml
        env:
          SEMGREP_APP_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.SEMGREP_APP_TOKEN }}

  secret-scan:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
        with:
          fetch-depth: 0
      - name: Trufflehog scan
        uses: trufflesecurity/trufflehog@main
        with:
          path: ./
          base: ${{ github.event.repository.default_branch }}
          head: HEAD
          extra_args: --include-paths=.trufflehog-include --only-verified

  dependency-cve:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Install pip-audit
        run: pip install pip-audit
      - name: Audit dependencies
        run: pip-audit --requirement requirements.txt --format json -o audit.json
      - name: Fail on high CVE
        run: python scripts/ci/check-cve-severity.py audit.json --max-severity high

  adversarial-eval:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    if: |
      github.event.pull_request.changed_files != '' &&
      (contains(join(github.event.pull_request.changed_files, ','), 'prompts/') ||
       contains(join(github.event.pull_request.changed_files, ','), 'tools/'))
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Run adversarial eval suite
        run: python scripts/ci/run-adversarial-eval.py

# .semgrep/agentic-rules.yml
rules:
  - id: prompt-user-input-concat
    patterns:
      - pattern: |
          ... + $USER_INPUT + ...
      - pattern-inside: |
          $PROMPT = ...
      - metavariable-regex:
          metavariable: $USER_INPUT
          regex: (user_input|user_message|user_query|request_body)
    message: "Direct user input concatenation in prompt — use template sanitisation"
    languages: [python]
    severity: ERROR

  - id: tool-definition-wildcard-scope
    pattern: |
      {"name": "...", "parameters": {"properties": {}, ...}}
    message: "Tool definition with empty parameters may indicate over-permissive scope"
    languages: [json]
    severity: WARNING

  - id: llm-output-to-eval
    patterns:
      - pattern: eval($LLM_OUTPUT)
      - pattern: exec($LLM_OUTPUT)
    message: "LLM output passed directly to eval/exec — severe injection risk"
    languages: [python]
    severity: ERROR
Phase 3: Adversarial eval as CI gate

# scripts/ci/run-adversarial-eval.py
# Runs the adversarial prompt suite and exits non-zero if pass rate < threshold.
import json, subprocess, sys

THRESHOLD = 0.95  # 95% pass rate required
SUITE_PATH = "evals/adversarial/"

def run_eval_suite():
    subprocess.run(
        ["python", "-m", "pytest", SUITE_PATH,
         "--json-report", "--json-report-file=eval-results.json", "-q"],
        check=False
    )
    with open("eval-results.json") as f:
        report = json.load(f)

    total = report["summary"]["total"]
    passed = report["summary"]["passed"]
    failed = report["summary"]["failed"]
    pass_rate = passed / total if total > 0 else 0

    print(f"Adversarial eval: {passed}/{total} passed ({pass_rate:.1%})")
    print(f"Required threshold: {THRESHOLD:.1%}")

    if pass_rate < THRESHOLD:
        print(f"GATE FAILED: {failed} adversarial prompts bypassed defences")
        for test in report["tests"]:
            if test["outcome"] == "failed":
                print(f"  FAILED: {test['nodeid']}")
        sys.exit(1)
    print("GATE PASSED")

run_eval_suite()

# scripts/security/prompt_scanner.py
import re
from pathlib import Path

DANGEROUS_PATTERNS = [
    (r'\{\s*user_input\s*\}', 'raw user_input interpolation'),
    (r'\{\s*message\s*\}',    'raw message interpolation'),
    (r'\+\s*(user|request|query)', 'string concat with user data'),
]

def check_prompt_file(filepath: str) -> list[dict]:
    text = Path(filepath).read_text()
    findings = []
    for i, line in enumerate(text.splitlines(), 1):
        for pattern, desc in DANGEROUS_PATTERNS:
            if re.search(pattern, line):
                findings.append({
                    "file": filepath, "line": i,
                    "description": desc,
                    "content": line.strip(),
                    "severity": "HIGH",
                })
    return findings

# Vulnerable prompt template (do NOT use in production)
EXAMPLE_VULNERABLE = (
    "You are a helpful assistant. Answer this user question:\n"
    "{user_input}\n"
    "Take action based on what the user asks."
)

# Safe version: pre-validated, scoped fields only
EXAMPLE_SAFE = (
    "You are a helpful assistant. "
    "The user account type is {account_type}. "
    "Answer the pre-validated question: {validated_query}"
)

# tests/security/test_gate_integration.py
import pytest
from pathlib import Path
from scripts.security.prompt_scanner import check_prompt_file
from scripts.security.tool_scope_checker import audit_tool_definitions

def test_no_injection_in_production_prompts():
    prompt_files = (
        list(Path("src/prompts").glob("**/*.txt")) +
        list(Path("src/prompts").glob("**/*.jinja2"))
    )
    all_findings = []
    for pf in prompt_files:
        all_findings.extend(check_prompt_file(str(pf)))
    high_severity = [f for f in all_findings if f["severity"] == "HIGH"]
    assert not high_severity, f"Injection vulnerabilities: {high_severity}"

def test_tool_definitions_scoped():
    findings = audit_tool_definitions("src/tools/")
    assert not findings, f"Over-permissive tool definitions: {findings}"

def test_adversarial_eval_threshold():
    import subprocess, json
    subprocess.run(
        ["python", "-m", "pytest", "evals/adversarial/",
         "--json-report", "--json-report-file=/tmp/adv-eval.json", "-q"],
        check=False
    )
    with open("/tmp/adv-eval.json") as f:
        r = json.load(f)
    rate = r["summary"]["passed"] / r["summary"]["total"]
    assert rate >= 0.95, f"Adversarial eval pass rate {rate:.1%} below 95% threshold"

A financial services firm operating a customer-facing document analysis agent deployed a five-gate security pipeline after discovering that a developer had embedded a staging-environment API key in a system prompt template file. The key was committed, pushed, and merged before anyone noticed — discovered only when automated secret rotation flagged the key as active outside expected service accounts.

The retrofitted pipeline added gitleaks with a custom pattern for their internal API key format, Semgrep rules for prompt file injection patterns, and a mandatory security reviewer approval. The adversarial eval suite was built from the red-team exercises the security team had previously run manually, now automated as 47 pytest cases covering jailbreak attempts, indirect injection via document content, and tool-call manipulation prompts.

Within the first 60 days of operation, the pipeline caught: 3 instances of test credentials in prompt files, 1 tool definition with write access where read-only was intended, and 2 Semgrep violations where user message content was interpolated into a downstream SQL query string template without sanitisation. All were caught pre-merge and remediated before reaching production.
Prompt templates, configuration YAML, and JSON tool definition files contain as much security risk as Python source. Gates must cover all file types.
Treating false positives as acceptable noise:
When developers learn to ignore gate failures due to frequent false positives, the gate loses its blocking effect. Rule tuning is as important as initial deployment.
Human approval without a structured checklist:
A generic security review approved note without a checklist produces inconsistent coverage across different reviewers and different shifts.
Adversarial eval suite that never grows:
A suite built once and never updated does not cover new attack patterns. Red-team findings must be converted into new eval cases as they are discovered.
Emergency exemptions to bypass a security gate are sometimes necessary but must be tracked with expiry dates. Permanent exemptions accumulate and erode coverage.
Automated gates provide consistent fast coverage for known patterns. Manual gates provide judgment for novel threat surfaces. Both are required.
Condition the adversarial eval job on file path patterns so it runs only when prompt templates or tool definitions change, keeping the gate fast for unrelated changes.
Measure gate pass rate, false positive rate, and time-to-resolve-gate-failure weekly. Declining pass rate indicates rule quality issues; rising resolution time indicates developer friction.
Every red-team exercise that discovers a new vulnerability class should produce a Semgrep rule or adversarial eval case within one sprint of the exercise completion.
Not all security findings should block merge. Separate HIGH severity (blocking) from LOW severity (informational) so developers address critical issues without being overwhelmed.

The gate pipeline must run within a latency budget that keeps developer flow reasonable. A pipeline longer than 20 minutes per PR creates significant friction; beyond 30 minutes causes developers to context-switch and return to the PR later, breaking the single-task focus needed for quality security remediation.

Parallelisation is the primary latency management tool: SAST scan, secret detection, dependency CVE check, and adversarial eval can run as independent parallel jobs. The human approval gate is available as soon as all automated gates pass, so the critical path is the longest single automated job — typically adversarial eval.

For large repositories, path-based job triggering reduces unnecessary gate runs: adversarial eval runs only when prompt templates or tool definitions change; SAST runs on all Python changes; secret detection runs on all file changes. This reduces gate latency for PRs that touch unrelated code while maintaining full coverage for risky changes.

The ecosystem for agentic AI-specific security tooling is still maturing. Semgrep's community ruleset has limited agentic-specific coverage; teams must author custom rules. The adversarial eval gate has no off-the-shelf solution and must be built from the team's own red-team exercises and incident retrospectives.

Runtime guardrails and WAF-style prompt injection filters are complementary to pre-merge security gates, not substitutes. Runtime detection catches attacks that reach production; pre-merge gates prevent vulnerabilities from entering the codebase. Both layers are required in a mature security posture for agentic AI.

The security gate pipeline is itself a security-critical system. If the gate can be bypassed — by disabling branch protection rules, by self-approving a PR that also modifies the gate configuration, or by modifying CI configuration in the same PR that introduces a vulnerability — the entire gate framework is undermined.

Protect the gate pipeline by requiring two-person approval for any change to branch protection rules or CI security job configuration; using a CODEOWNERS file that routes security gate configuration changes to the security team; and auditing any change to the security gate workflow files with the same scrutiny as changes to production secrets management systems.
Require status checks from specific CI jobs; do not allow administrators to bypass required checks even in emergency scenarios.
Route any change to .semgrep/, .github/workflows/security-gates.yml, and evals/adversarial/ to the security team for approval.
Log any instance where a security gate was bypassed (even by administrators) and review in the next weekly security standup.
Store Semgrep rules and adversarial eval suites in a read-only location for standard developers, modifiable only through a separate protected PR process.

Think of security review gates as a series of progressively more expert checkpoints at a border crossing. The first checkpoint is automated document scanning (SAST, secrets) — fast, consistent, runs on every traveller. The second checkpoint is an X-ray machine (dependency CVE scan) that identifies known dangerous items. The third checkpoint is an adversarial scenario test (red-team eval). The final checkpoint is a customs officer (human security reviewer) who applies judgment to cases that automated scanning cannot evaluate.

A traveller who bypasses the first checkpoint undermines the security of the entire border. The gate is only as strong as its enforcement: automated checks must be required status checks with no bypass mechanism for urgency, and human approval must require a completed structured checklist rather than a general review note.

Security review gates for agentic AI must extend standard SAST and secret scanning with AI-specific checks: prompt injection analysis, tool scope review, and adversarial eval suites run on every PR that touches prompts or tools.
Gates must be technically enforced through required CI status checks and branch protection rules, not socially enforced through review processes that can be bypassed under delivery pressure.
The adversarial eval suite functions as a regression test for security: every red-team finding that discovers a new vulnerability class should become a permanent CI test case within one sprint.
Human approval gates require a structured checklist covering threat model delta, permission scope, and output handling — not a general approval note — to produce consistent and auditable coverage.
The gate pipeline is itself a security-critical system; protect it with CODEOWNERS routing, two-person approval for configuration changes, and bypass logging.
Runtime guardrails are complementary to pre-merge gates, not substitutes: both layers are required in a production agentic AI security posture.
Track gate metrics weekly (pass rate, false positive rate, resolution time) to detect rule quality degradation and developer friction before they erode gate effectiveness.
